content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September, 27, 1994

Mr. James R. Sutton
Nielsen, Merksamer, Parrinello,
Mueller & Naylor
591 Redwood Highway, #4000
Mill Valley, CA 94941

Re: Confirmation of Telephone Advice
Our File No. I-94-287

Dear Mr. Sutton:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1/ Because your question is general in nature, we
will treat your letter as a request for informal assistance
pursuant to Regulation 18239(c).2/

Your letter, dated August 31, 1994, accurately summarizes my
advice that the provisions of Regulation 18233 only apply to
employees or to independent contractors acting as full-time
employees. (Terry Advice Letter, No. A-84-164.) Therefore, if a
company retains a consultant to work on a political campaign, all
of the consultant's time must be considered an expenditure and,
depending upon the circumstances, a nonmonetary contribution.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-287
Page 2


If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,


Lynda Cassady
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

sections.conclusion: The provisions of Regulation 18423 only apply to employees or to independent contractors acting as full-time employees. Therefore, if a company retains a consultant to work on a political campaign, all of the consultant's time must be considered an expenditure and, depending upon the circumstances, a nonmonetary contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether consultant time working on political campaigns is subject to campaign expenditure and contribution requirements, specifically whether Regulation 18423 applies to consultants retained by companies.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC confirms that Regulation 18423 applies only to employees or full-time independent contractors. All time spent by a consultant retained by a company to work on a political campaign must be considered an expenditure and potentially a nonmonetary contribution.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a confirmation of prior telephone advice letter. No formal Question/Conclusion sections present, but the advice and conclusion are clearly stated in the body of the letter. Document is treated as informal assistance per Regulation 18239(c), which means it does not provide immunity like formal written advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether consultant time working on political campaigns is subject to campaign expenditure and contribution requirements, specifically whether Regulation 18423 applies to consultants retained by companies.

CONCLUSION: The provisions of Regulation 18423 only apply to employees or to independent contractors acting as full-time employees. Therefore, if a company retains a consultant to work on a political campaign, all of the consultant's time must be considered an expenditure and, depending upon the circumstances, a nonmonetary contribution.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice regarding whether consultant time on political campaigns constitutes campaign expenditures and contributions under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC advises that Regulation 18423 applies only to employees or full-time independent contractors, so consultant time must be treated as an expenditure and nonmonetary contribution.
